title: Memory leak in v2.3 real-time notification system causes progressive slowdown requiring daily restart
problem: After upgrading from v2.2 to v2.3, the TaskFlow server leaks memory proportional to user activity. Memory climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ over a workday with 200 active users, causing 10+ second page loads and API timeouts. Memory never reclaims — it only grows during activity and plateaus during idle periods. A full server restart is the only way to recover.
root_cause_hypothesis: The v2.3 changes to the real-time notifications system introduced a memory leak where per-user or per-event data structures are allocated on each interaction but never freed when users disconnect or events are delivered. The fact that established connection counts remain normal (ruling out socket/connection leaks) while memory only grows and never reclaims points to an in-process data structure (e.g., an event history buffer, subscriber registry, or notification queue) that accumulates entries without eviction or cleanup.
reproduction_steps: Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 on a test VM with real-time notifications enabled
Simulate 50-200 concurrent users performing normal task operations (creating, updating, completing tasks) over several hours
Monitor process memory (RSS) — it should climb steadily and never decrease even as simulated users disconnect
Compare with the same test on v2.2 to confirm the regression
environment: TaskFlow v2.3, self-hosted on 8GB RAM VM running Ubuntu 22.04, ~200 active users, real-time browser push notifications enabled, no external integrations (no Slack, email, or webhooks)
impact: All 200 active users experience progressive degradation daily. By late afternoon the application is effectively unusable (10+ second loads, API timeouts). Operations team must restart the server every evening. Productivity loss is significant and ongoing.
recommended_fix: Diff the real-time notification subsystem between v2.2 and v2.3. Look for in-memory collections (subscriber maps, event buffers, notification queues) that grow per-user or per-event without cleanup. Likely candidates: (1) a notification history or event log that appends but never trims, (2) a subscriber/session registry that adds entries on connect but doesn't remove them on disconnect, or (3) per-user message queues that are allocated but never garbage-collected. Add proper cleanup on WebSocket/SSE disconnect and consider bounded data structures (e.g., ring buffers or TTL-based eviction) for any event history.
proposed_test_case: Write a load test that connects N simulated users, has them perform actions generating notifications, then disconnects them. Assert that after all users disconnect and a GC cycle is forced, server memory returns to within a small margin of baseline. Run this for multiple connect/disconnect cycles to verify no accumulation.
information_gaps: No heap dump or memory profiler output to pinpoint the exact leaking object type
Haven't confirmed whether rolling back to v2.2 resolves the issue (though timing correlation is strong)
Unknown whether the leak rate scales linearly with user count or with notification volume specifically
